title: Ensure that the --kubeconfig kubelet.conf file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the --kubeconfig kubelet.conf file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The kubelet.conf file is the kubeconfig file for the node, and controls various parameters that set the behavior and identity of the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
input_row.audit: Automated AAC auditing has been modified to allow CIS-CAT to input a variable for the <PATH>/<FILENAME> of the kubelet config file. Please set $kubelet_config=<PATH> based on the file location on your system for example: export kubelet_config=/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf To perform the audit manually: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf Default Value: By default, kubelet.conf file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/
input_row.profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Worker Node •  Level 2 - Worker Node

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control requires verification of the file permissions and ownership of the kubelet.conf file on each worker node.
- This information (Linux file permissions and ownership) is not exposed via the Kubernetes API, kubectl, or any Kubernetes-native resource or controller.
- Verification requires direct access to the node’s filesystem (e.g., via SSH), which is outside the scope of Kubernetes API or kubectl capabilities.
- Therefore, this control cannot be automated via Kubernetes APIs and must be audited manually.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node.
2. Identify the location of the kubelet.conf file (commonly /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf, but may vary).
3. Run the following command to check the file permissions and ownership:
   ```
   stat -c "%a %U %G" /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf
   ```
4. Verify that:
   - The permissions are 644 or more restrictive (i.e., 644, 640, 600, etc.).
   - The ownership is set to root:root.
5. If both conditions are met, the node is compliant. If not, remediate as necessary (e.g., `chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf` and `chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf`).

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.7.1 PDF
